quote_of_day.text: >>21969 Stop caring about being made fun of. Own your inner locus. the "look us". Look at yourself. Maybe a few others. Get control of your life. White opinions don't matter and never did matter. They only grind your soul to dust and waste your time.
quote_of_day.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather for Indus today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of casteist remarks moved into the region, bringing scattered showers of slurs and racist comments.

Anon sparked a brief downpour of fantasies about serving in the Pakistani army and slaying 'dasyus'.

A high-pressure ridge of mocking queries formed over the board, with one anon posing the question 'Do you mog him?' - a brief but pointed inquiry.

Meanwhile, a cold front of decomposed gobar-related discussions blew in, marked by a flurry of posts tagged #GOBAR DECOMPOSED #DEGOBARD.

A lone voice suggested changing Indian accents to improve perception, but found little traction in the prevailing winds.

articles.title: Anon Suggests Changing Indian Accent to Improve Perception
articles.body:
Anon opened the discussion by suggesting that the Indian accent is a "break point that hurts us." They proposed exploring ways to reform or change the accent, citing colonial Indian accents as a potential reference point.

Anon responded with a seemingly unrelated comment about "racemixing pressures" and the potential consequences of interracial relationships. Another anon chimed in, suggesting that the issue is not just the accent, but also India's racial appearance.

The original poster elaborated on their idea, proposing that a small group of like-minded individuals could create cultural artifacts and trends that could help bring about change. Anon suggested that creating a new class of "cosmopolitan Indians" could provide a vehicle for progress.

The conversation took a different turn when Anon commented on the perceived motivations of "Germanics" and their alleged desire to "cuck" Asians.

Another anon responded by stating that changing one's accent or appearance is not a solution, and that individuals should focus on personal growth and financial success.

Anon argued that the issue is not just about the accent, but about how Indians are perceived globally. They suggested that creating a new cultural identity could help change this perception.

Anon responded by advising others to "stop caring" about what others think and focus on their own lives. The discussion also touched on the topic of interracial relationships and the potential consequences for individuals and society.

Anon claimed that "racemixed kids" have their own haplotype and haplogroup, and that they may face challenges in finding organ donors. The conversation concluded with various comments on the nature of identity, culture, and personal responsibility.

Anon suggested that individuals should focus on their own lives and not worry about what others think. Another anon argued that changing one's accent or appearance is not a solution, and that individuals should focus on personal growth and financial success.

articles.title: Anon Sparks Discussion on Decomposed Gobars
articles.body:
Anon opened the discussion with a post reading '#GOBAR DECOMPOSED #DEGOBARD'.

One respondent claimed that someone, presumably a woman, who went to the US got what was coming to her, suggesting it was her fault for moving to a country they described as 'a nigger shithole'.

Another poster sarcastically commented that decomposing gobars was a hot topic and joked about studying the existential dread of sentient trash heaps.

A different anon pushed back, stating that the woman was innocent and only trying to escape to a better country, characterizing India as 'dalit rashtra'.

One participant made a comment about the term 'gobar' possibly meaning 'go outside', and another joked that a better country would mean everyone stays indoors.

The discussion also involved several exchanges of insults and put-downs between various anons, with some using derogatory terms like 'dalit' and 'nigger', and others making personal attacks.

The tone of the conversation remained darkly comedic and critical throughout, with several posters responding with hostility or sarcasm to the initial post and to each other.

articles.title: Anon fantasises about serving in Pak army, slaying 'dasyus'
articles.body:
Anon expressed a desire to serve in the Pakistani army and 'slay down dasyus'.

Another anon responded with a claim to only kill low-caste individuals and certain groups they referred to as 'kallu madarchods', while sparing upper castes and Northeast Indians.

A respondent mocked the idea, saying that having certain lower-caste individuals serve while they 'fuck the spoils of war' would be more 'aryan', and that the previous poster, referred to as 'choti jaat', wouldn't understand.

One anon brought up a historical point, stating that certain groups originated during a time when caste was not strict and endogamy was not strictly practiced.

Another poster chimed in, saying they would only kill certain individuals if there were Rajputs, Sikhs, and Gorkhas present, referring to the latter groups as 'bro tier'.

A critic called out the original poster, saying they were 'brainwashed' to think that killing people makes one a hero. Anon also expressed a fantasy about being transported into early Aryavarta with their memories intact.

A supportive poster called the original post 'based asf', while another simply stated 'TOTAL BHIMRASHTRA DEATH'. Finally, an anon asked about the fate of 'kala bamans'.

articles.title: Anon describes Kerala trip, calls locals 'subhumans'
articles.body:
Anon shared a post about their daily encounters with North Indians, describing some as 'passable for gorims' with lighter eyes or hair.

They contrasted this with a trip to Kerala, where they claimed to have seen only 'dark, goblinoid, shitflinging Dravidian demons.' Another anon responded, calling Dravidian people 'inferior in every way' and crediting Brahmins for making South India 'somewhat livable.' A respondent challenged this view, saying 'beautiful, noble things' shouldn't negatively impact one's mindset.

Anon's comment about caste was met with brief responses. One simply said 'Caste?' while another posted 'Test.'

articles.title: Mogging Queries
articles.body: Anon asked if someone 'mogs' him. Anon responded, querying if the person in question is someone's 'first pick', implying a possible romantic or preferential context. Another Anon mentioned having no outdoor needs but potentially requiring a big car for their kids, jokingly concluding they would be 'moggable' if they're not the first choice. A separate Anon expressed confusion, asking what others were discussing.
